topics.title: Java Compilation & Execution Flow
topics.description: Trace the complete lifecycle of a Java program: from .java source through lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, bytecode generation (.class), ClassLoader, JIT compilation, to native CPU execution. Understand each transformation and why it matters.
topics.story: Imagine sending a letter internationally. You write it in English (source code). A translator (javac) converts it to a universal Esperanto (bytecode) that any country's postal service can handle. At the destination, a local postal worker (JVM) reads the Esperanto letter and delivers it in the local language (machine code) to the recipient (CPU). If the same route is used often (JIT), the postal worker memorizes the delivery and goes directly without re-reading the Esperanto each time.
topics.analogy: Building a house: Architect draws blueprints (source .java) → Blueprint reviewer checks for structural errors (javac semantic analysis) → Standardized blueprint format created (bytecode .class) → Construction foreman reads the blueprint (ClassLoader) → Workers build using local materials and tools (JIT to native) → Family lives in the house (CPU executes). Different cities (OS/architecture) use the same blueprint but different local materials.
topics.memoryAnchor: SPLICE: Source → Parse → Link(resolve) → Interpret/JIT → CPU Execute. Or simpler: '.java → javac → .class → JVM → CPU'. Three file types: .java (you write), .class (compiler produces), native code (JIT produces at runtime).
topics.firstPrinciples: Why not compile Java directly to machine code like C++? Because machine code is CPU-architecture specific (x86, ARM, RISC-V). Bytecode is a portable intermediate representation for a virtual stack machine — any CPU that runs a JVM implementation can execute the same .class file. The bytecode instruction set is deliberately simple (stack-based) to make JVM implementation straightforward across platforms.
topics.bruteForce: Direct compilation (C++ approach): compile once per target architecture. Shipping software = shipping multiple binaries (windows-x64.exe, linux-arm64, macos-m1). Build pipeline complexity multiplies. Cross-compilation requires special toolchains. A bug fix requires rebuilding and shipping all variants.
topics.optimizedApproach: Java's two-phase approach: (1) javac compiles to architecture-neutral bytecode once — the .class file is identical everywhere. (2) JVM does final translation to native code at runtime, specific to the local CPU. Single artifact, universal deployment. JIT further ensures native-level performance for hot code paths.
topics.whenToUse: Understanding compilation flow matters for: diagnosing 'compiled against Java 17, running on Java 11' errors (UnsupportedClassVersionError), understanding why annotation processors (Lombok, MapStruct) must run at compile time, knowing why reflection bypasses compile-time type checking, debugging incremental compilation issues in IDEs, and understanding why changing a single source file can force recompilation of dependent classes.

examples.title: Compilation Phases — What javac Does
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":1,"lineCode":"public class HelloCompile {","variables":{"phase":"Parsing"},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"Phase 1: Scanner tokenizes character stream. Phase 2: Parser builds ClassDecl AST node"},{"line":3,"lineCode":"String message = \"Hello\";","variables":{"phase":"SemanticAnalysis"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Phase 3: Symbol table records 'message: String'. Type checker verifies \"Hello\" is String literal"},{"line":4,"lineCode":"int count = 3;","variables":{"phase":"SemanticAnalysis"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Type checker: 3 is int literal, compatible with int declaration. No implicit cast needed"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"for (int i = 0; i < count; i++)","variables":{"phase":"SemanticAnalysis"},"phase":"LOOP_START","annotation":"Scope analysis: 'i' declared in for-init scope. count resolved from enclosing block. i < count: int < int = boolean ✓"},{"line":6,"lineCode":"System.out.println(message + \" #\" + i);","variables":{"phase":"BytecodeGen"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Bytecode gen: StringBuilder created for string concat. Emits: new StringBuilder, append(message), append(\" #\"), append(i), toString(), invokevirtual println"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[.java Source] -->|Phase 1: Lexer| B[Token Stream]
    B -->|Phase 2: Parser| C[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    C -->|Phase 3: Semantic Analysis| D[Annotated AST]
    D -->|Phase 4: Code Gen| E[.class Bytecode]
    E -->|ClassLoader| F[JVM Method Area]
    F -->|Interpreter| G[Execution]
    F -->|JIT Compiler| H[Native Code]
    H --> G
examples.pseudocode:
javac pipeline:
  source.java
    -> tokenize() -> [Token...]
    -> parse()    -> AST
    -> analyze()  -> TypeChecked AST
    -> generate() -> .class file

java pipeline:
  .class file
    -> ClassLoader.loadClass()
    -> JVM interprets bytecodes
    -> JIT compiles hot methods
    -> CPU executes native code
examples.explanation: Each javac phase transforms the representation. Lexical errors (invalid character) caught in phase 1. Syntax errors (missing semicolon) caught in phase 2. Type errors (String + int incompatibility) caught in phase 3. Phase 4 is 'just' code emission — all correctness checks are done. This is why Java can catch errors like 'variable might not have been initialized' at compile time — the dataflow analysis runs in phase 3.
examples.realWorldUse: Annotation processors (Lombok @Data, MapStruct @Mapper, Jakarta Bean Validation @Valid) plug into phase 3/4 of javac via the javax.annotation.processing API. They inspect the AST and generate new .java files which then go through the full pipeline. This is why Lombok can generate constructors/getters at compile time without runtime overhead.

examples.title: UnsupportedClassVersionError — Cross-Version Compilation
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"int classFileMajorVersion = getClassFileVersion(VersionDemo.class);","variables":{"classFileMajorVersion":0},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Calling getClassFileVersion — reads the .class file bytes directly"},{"line":22,"lineCode":"var is = clazz.getResourceAsStream(...)","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"getResourceAsStream reads the .class file from classpath as raw bytes"},{"line":25,"lineCode":"byte[] bytes = is.readAllBytes();","variables":{"bytes":"[0xCA,0xFE,0xBA,0xBE,0x00,0x00,0x3D,0x00,...]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"First 4 bytes: 0xCAFEBABE (Java magic number). Bytes 4-5: minor version. Bytes 6-7: major version"},{"line":27,"lineCode":"return ((bytes[6] & 0xFF) << 8) | (bytes[7] & 0xFF);","variables":{"result":61},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"For Java 17: major version = 61 (0x3D). 61 - 44 = 17. JVM rejects if this exceeds its max supported version"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[javac Java 21] -->|emits class file version 65| B[VersionDemo.class]
    B --> C{JVM checks version}
    C -->|JVM Java 21, supports up to 65| D[OK - runs]
    C -->|JVM Java 11, supports up to 55| E[UnsupportedClassVersionError!]
    
    F[Fix: compile with --release 11] -->|emits class file version 55| G[Compatible .class]
    G --> H[Runs on Java 11+]
examples.pseudocode:
.class file header:
  bytes 0-3:  0xCAFEBABE  (magic number)
  bytes 4-5:  minor version
  bytes 6-7:  major version
  
JVM at load time:
  if majorVersion > JVM.supportedMax:
    throw UnsupportedClassVersionError
  
Fix: javac --release 11 VersionDemo.java
  forces bytecode compatible with Java 11 JVM
examples.explanation: The --release flag (preferred over -source/-target) restricts use of APIs added after the target version AND emits the correct class file version. This is the correct way to ship libraries that support multiple Java versions. Without it: compile on developer's Java 21 JDK, deploy to production Java 11 server = crash.
examples.realWorldUse: Library authors (Spring Boot, Apache Commons) compile with --release 17 to support Java 17+ while using Java 17 APIs. Maven Compiler Plugin: <release>17</release>. If you're writing a library used by others, always test that your build.gradle/pom.xml sets the correct target version — it's the #1 cause of 'works on my machine' deployment failures.

examples.title: Annotation Processing at Compile Time
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":8,"lineCode":"@Retention(RetentionPolicy.SOURCE)","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"RetentionPolicy.SOURCE: annotation discarded after compilation. Not in .class file. Compile-time only tool"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"class TimedProcessor extends AbstractProcessor","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"javac discovers processors via META-INF/services/javax.annotation.processing.Processor service file"},{"line":18,"lineCode":"for (Element el : roundEnv.getElementsAnnotatedWith(Timed.class))","variables":{"annotations":"[@Timed]"},"phase":"LOOP_START","annotation":"javac calls process() for each round. roundEnv contains all elements annotated with @Timed in this compilation unit"},{"line":21,"lineCode":"processingEnv.getMessager().printMessage(...)","variables":{"element":"fetchData"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Real processors use Filer to generate new .java files. Those new files re-enter the compilation pipeline in next round"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[.java source with @Timed] -->|Round 1| B[javac parses AST]
    B --> C[Annotation Processor triggered]
    C -->|generates new .java files?| D{New files?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Round 2: compile generated files]
    D -->|No| F[Continue to bytecode generation]
    E --> B
    F --> G[.class output]
examples.pseudocode:
javac + annotation processing rounds:
  Round 1:
    parse all .java files
    call processor.process() for matching annotations
    processor may generate new .java files
  Round 2 (if new files generated):
    parse new .java files
    call processors again
  Final round (no new files):
    emit bytecode for all classes
examples.explanation: Annotation processing is compile-time code generation. Lombok uses this to generate getters/setters/constructors — zero runtime overhead. MapStruct generates type-safe mapper implementations. Jakarta Bean Validation generates validation classes. The processor runs inside javac, so errors in generated code appear as normal compile errors.
examples.realWorldUse: In Spring Boot projects, @SpringBootApplication triggers component scanning at startup (runtime). But compile-time tools like MapStruct @Mapper generate mapper .java files during mvn compile — the generated code is plain Java with no reflection, making it 10-100x faster than reflection-based mappers like ModelMapper.

examples.title: String Concatenation Compilation Evolution
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":6,"lineCode":"String result = \"Hello \" + name + \", count=\" + count;","variables":{"name":"World","count":42},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Java 8: javac emits new StringBuilder() + 4x append() calls. Java 9+: single invokedynamic instruction"},{"line":25,"lineCode":"int major = Integer.parseInt(javaVersion.split(\"[.]\")[0]);","variables":{"javaVersion":"17.0.2","major":17},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Parse major version: '17.0.2'.split('[.]')[0] = '17'. parseInt = 17"},{"line":26,"lineCode":"if (major >= 9)","variables":{"major":17},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"major=17 >= 9: true branch taken. Java 9+ path: invokedynamic is used"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[String + concat in source] --> B{Java Version?}
    B -->|Java 8| C[javac emits StringBuilder chain]
    B -->|Java 9+| D[javac emits invokedynamic]
    
    C --> E[Runtime: StringBuilder.append() calls]
    D --> F[Runtime: StringConcatFactory chooses strategy]
    F --> G[byte[] direct copy - fastest]
    F --> H[StringBuilder - fallback]
    
    G --> I[Result String]
    H --> I
    E --> I
examples.pseudocode:
Java 8 bytecode for "a" + b + "c":
  new StringBuilder
  append("a")
  append(b)
  append("c")
  toString()

Java 9+ bytecode:
  invokedynamic StringConcatFactory.makeConcatWithConstants
    template: "a\u0001c" (\u0001 = argument placeholder)
    args: [b]
    -> JVM generates optimized concat at first call
examples.explanation: The invokedynamic (indy) instruction is the JVM's extension point for dynamic dispatch. StringConcatFactory bootstraps a method handle at first call that performs the concat. This allows JVM vendors to swap in faster concat strategies (e.g., using direct byte array manipulation) without changing the bytecode. It's also how lambdas work — lambda invocations use indy + LambdaMetafactory.
examples.realWorldUse: This is why benchmarks that test String concatenation must run with proper warm-up — the LambdaMetafactory and StringConcatFactory bootstrap calls only happen once (at first indy call). Understanding indy is critical for understanding why lambda creation is fast after first use: the Method Handle is cached and reused.

examples.title: Decompiling .class Back to Source
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":6,"lineCode":"public BeforeDecompile(String name, int value) {","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"Constructor compiled to <init> method in bytecode. First instruction always calls super.<init> (invokespecial)"},{"line":7,"lineCode":"this.name = name;","variables":{"this":"ref","name":"param"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"aload_0 (this), aload_1 (name param), putfield — stores into this.name field"},{"line":8,"lineCode":"this.value = value;","variables":{"this":"ref","value":"param"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"aload_0 (this), iload_2 (value param, integer uses iload not aload), putfield"},{"line":12,"lineCode":"return name + \"=\" + value;","variables":{},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Java 9+: invokedynamic for concat. Java 8: new StringBuilder + appends. Returns String reference"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[BeforeDecompile.java] -->|javac| B[BeforeDecompile.class]
    B -->|javap -c| C[Human-readable bytecode]
    B -->|CFR/Fernflower decompiler| D[Reconstructed .java]
    
    subgraph .class contains
      E[Class metadata]
      F[Field descriptors]
      G[Method bytecode]
      H[Constant pool]
      I[Debug info: line numbers, variable names]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
javap flags:
  -c       : show bytecode instructions
  -p       : show private members
  -verbose : show constant pool, stack sizes
  -l       : show line number table
  
java decompilers:
  CFR (jar):         java -jar cfr.jar MyClass.class
  Fernflower (IDEA): built into IntelliJ IDEA
  Procyon:           good for Java 8 lambdas
  
What .class preserves:
  + field/method names and types
  + method bodies as bytecode
  + debug line number tables (if compiled with -g)
  - comments (stripped)
  - local variable names (if no -g flag)
examples.explanation: Decompilers can reconstruct surprisingly readable source from .class files. This is why obfuscation tools (ProGuard, R8) rename classes/methods to gibberish (a, b, c) before shipping production JARs. The bytecode logic is still there, but without meaningful names it's much harder to understand. Android apps use R8 for this — that's why decompiled APKs look like 'a.a.a.b()'.
examples.realWorldUse: Debugging production issues without source: use IntelliJ's built-in decompiler (navigate to .class in External Libraries section — IDEA decompiles automatically). Or use javap -c to view bytecode when you need to verify that the JIT is compiling what you think, or to understand why two seemingly identical code paths perform differently.

problems.title: Predict Compilation Error or Success
problems.description:
For each code snippet, predict if it will compile successfully or fail with a compilation error, and if it fails, name the javac phase that catches it:

A: `int x = "hello";`
B: `String s = null; s.length();`
C: `class Foo { Foo() {} } Foo f = new Foo(42);`
D: `int y = 10 / 0;`
E: `@interface X {} @X @X void method() {}`
problems.hint1: Type errors (A) are caught in semantic analysis phase. Runtime errors (B) compile fine but throw at runtime.
problems.hint2: D (division by zero) is a runtime error — the compiler does not evaluate arithmetic expressions for runtime exceptions.
problems.hint3: E fails if @X is not marked @Repeatable. Without @Repeatable, applying the same annotation twice is a compile error.
problems.constraints: Analyze statically. Do not compile and run the snippets.
problems.editorial: A: int x = 'hello' — type mismatch, compile error. B: final int x = 5; int[] arr = new int[x] — final constants allowed as array size, compiles. C: switch on String — legal since Java 7. D: @Override on interface default — legal since Java 8. E: duplicate annotation without @Repeatable — compile error.

problems.title: Read Class File Magic Number
problems.description: Write a method `isJavaClassFile(byte[] fileBytes)` that returns true if the byte array starts with the Java class file magic number (0xCAFEBABE). Also write `getClassFileMajorVersion(byte[] fileBytes)` that extracts the major version from bytes 6-7.
problems.hint1: Java class files start with 4 magic bytes: 0xCA, 0xFE, 0xBA, 0xBE.
problems.hint2: Major version is at bytes index 6-7 (big-endian). Use bit shifting: (bytes[6] & 0xFF) << 8 | (bytes[7] & 0xFF).
problems.hint3: Java 17 = major version 61, Java 11 = 55, Java 8 = 52. Formula: javaVersion = majorVersion - 44.
problems.constraints: Handle null and short arrays. Use bit masking (& 0xFF) to treat bytes as unsigned.
problems.editorial: Java class files start with magic number 0xCAFEBABE (bytes [0xCA, 0xFE, 0xBA, 0xBE]). Major version is at bytes [6,7] as big-endian short. Java 8 = 52, 11 = 55, 17 = 61, 21 = 65. Formula: javaVersion = majorVersion - 44. Check fileBytes.length >= 8 before reading version.

problems.title: Compile Java Source at Runtime
problems.description: Write a method `compileAndRun(String className, String sourceCode)` that uses the javax.tools.JavaCompiler API to compile a String of Java source code at runtime, then loads and runs the compiled class.
problems.hint1: Use ToolProvider.getSystemJavaCompiler() to get the JavaCompiler instance. Requires JDK (not JRE).
problems.hint2: Write the source code to a temp .java file, compile it using compiler.run(), then load with URLClassLoader.
problems.hint3: After compilation, use URLClassLoader pointing to the temp directory. Call Class.forName() then invoke the main method via reflection.
problems.constraints: Requires JDK (ToolProvider.getSystemJavaCompiler() returns null in JRE). Must clean up temp files.
problems.editorial: JavaCompiler compiler = ToolProvider.getSystemJavaCompiler() — returns null if not running on JDK (only JRE). Write source to a temp .java file, compile with compiler.run(), then load with URLClassLoader pointing to the temp dir. Use reflection to call main() on the loaded class.

problems.title: Constant Pool Size Counter
problems.description: The constant pool in a .class file stores all string literals, class names, and method references. Write a method that reads a .class file and counts how many constant pool entries it contains. The constant pool count is stored as a 2-byte big-endian value at bytes 8-9 of the .class file.
problems.hint1: After the magic number (4 bytes) and version numbers (4 bytes), bytes 8-9 hold the constant pool count.
problems.hint2: The actual pool has (count - 1) entries — the count is one-indexed (slots start at 1, not 0).
problems.hint3: Read the file bytes with Files.readAllBytes() then extract bytes 8-9 as big-endian short.

testCases.input: valid .class bytes with count=25 at bytes 8-9

problems.constraints: Read bytes 8-9 as big-endian 2-byte unsigned value. Handle short arrays gracefully.
problems.editorial: The constant pool count is stored as a big-endian unsigned short at bytes 8-9 (0-indexed) in the .class file. Read ((bytes[8] & 0xFF) << 8) | (bytes[9] & 0xFF). The count is one more than the actual number of entries (entry 0 is reserved). Check minimum length of 10 bytes first.

problems.title: Source Retention vs Runtime Retention Annotation
problems.description: Demonstrate the three annotation retention policies by creating three annotations (SOURCE, CLASS, RUNTIME) and showing which ones are accessible via reflection at runtime.
problems.hint1: RetentionPolicy.SOURCE: stripped by javac. RetentionPolicy.CLASS: in .class but not loaded into JVM. RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME: available via reflection.
problems.hint2: Use Method.getAnnotation(AnnotationClass.class) — returns null if annotation not retained at runtime.
problems.hint3: SOURCE annotations are used by annotation processors (compile-time tools). RUNTIME annotations are used by frameworks like Spring and JUnit.
problems.constraints: Must define all three retention policies. getAnnotation() must return null for SOURCE and CLASS annotations.
problems.editorial: SOURCE annotations are discarded after compilation — not in .class file. CLASS annotations are in .class but not loaded at runtime. RUNTIME annotations are loaded and accessible via reflection. Spring's @Autowired, JUnit's @Test are RUNTIME. Lombok's @Data is SOURCE (annotation processor).

problems.title: Dead Code Elimination by javac
problems.description:
Java's compiler performs a simple form of dead code elimination for unreachable code after unconditional jumps. Write examples demonstrating:
1. Code after `return` — compile error (unreachable)
2. Code inside `if (false)` — allowed (compile-time constant folding)
3. Code inside `if (true)` — allowed, unreachable else branch kept as dead code

Explain why `if (false)` is allowed (debug pattern) but `return; statement;` is a compile error.
problems.hint1: javac performs reachability analysis per the JLS (Java Language Specification). Code after `return` is definitively unreachable.
problems.hint2: `if (false)` is a historical exception — allows the 'debug mode' pattern: `static final boolean DEBUG = false; if (DEBUG) { ... }`. The compiler keeps the class file structure but emits no code for the dead branch.
problems.hint3: `if (true)` makes the else branch unreachable. javac detects this and the else branch gets no bytecode, but it's not an error.
problems.constraints: DEBUG must be a static final boolean constant. Verify with javap that dead blocks emit no bytecode.
problems.editorial: if (DEBUG) where DEBUG is a compile-time constant (static final boolean = false) — javac eliminates the dead branch entirely, generating no bytecode for it. This is the 'compile-time conditional compilation' pattern. The else branch is included. Unreachable code after return/throw IS a compile error.

problems.title: Build a Simple Tokenizer (Lexer Phase)
problems.description:
Implement a simplified Java lexer that tokenizes a string of Java source code. Support these token types: KEYWORD (public/class/int/void/if/return), IDENTIFIER, NUMBER, STRING, OPERATOR (+/-/*/=/==/!=/</>), PUNCTUATION ({/}/(/)/;/,), WHITESPACE (skip).

Input: `"int x = 42;"`
Output: `[NUMBER_TOKEN(int), IDENTIFIER(x), OPERATOR(=), NUMBER(42), PUNCTUATION(;)]`
problems.hint1: Use a while loop with an index pointer scanning the input character by character.
problems.hint2: For each character: check if it's a letter (start of keyword/identifier), digit (number), quote (string), or symbol (operator/punctuation).
problems.hint3: For keywords vs identifiers: first collect the word, then check if it's in a predefined keyword set.
problems.constraints: Handle all listed token types. Skip whitespace. Distinguish keywords from identifiers. Support == and != as 2-char operators.
problems.editorial: Scan character by character. Track state: inside string literal, inside comment. Collect word chars for KEYWORD/IDENTIFIER — check against keyword set after collecting. For strings, collect everything between quotes. Operators and punctuation are single characters. Return List<Token> in order.

problems.title: AST Node Implementation
problems.description:
Implement a simple Abstract Syntax Tree for arithmetic expressions. Support: NumberNode (leaf), BinaryOpNode (+, -, *, /), and UnaryNegNode. Write an `evaluate()` method and a `toSource()` method that reconstructs the expression as a string.

Represent and evaluate: `-(3 + 4) * 2` → `-14`
problems.hint1: Use a sealed interface or abstract class for the Node type. Each concrete type implements evaluate() and toSource().
problems.hint2: BinaryOpNode has left and right child nodes plus an operator char (+,-,*,/). evaluate() recursively evaluates children.
problems.hint3: UnaryNegNode wraps one child. evaluate() returns -child.evaluate(). toSource() returns '(-' + child.toSource() + ')'.
problems.constraints: Must use composition (AST nodes reference other nodes). evaluate() must be recursive. Support +, -, *, / operators.
problems.editorial: Sealed interface AstNode with record implementations is clean. evaluate() recurses — BinaryOpNode evaluates both children, applies operator. Division by zero: throw ArithmeticException. toSource() wraps children in parentheses for unambiguous output. Use pattern matching switch for operators.

problems.title: Incremental Compilation Dependency Graph
problems.description:
Build a simple dependency graph that represents which Java classes depend on which others (like javac incremental compilation tracking). When class A changes, find all classes that need recompilation.

Implement: `addDependency(String classA, String classB)` (A depends on B) and `getAffectedByChange(String changedClass)` (returns all classes that need recompilation if changedClass changes).
problems.hint1: Use a Map<String, Set<String>> as an adjacency list. Key = class, Value = set of classes that depend ON it (reverse dependency).
problems.hint2: getAffectedByChange does a BFS/DFS from changedClass following reverse dependencies.
problems.hint3: Include the changed class itself in the result. Avoid infinite loops with a visited set.
problems.constraints: BFS/DFS must handle transitive dependencies. Avoid infinite loops. Include the changed class in the result.
problems.editorial: Build a reverse graph: for each 'A depends on B', store B -> A. BFS/DFS from the changed class on the reverse graph finds all classes that transitively depend on it. Include the changed class in results. Use a visited set to avoid cycles. This is how Maven Reactor and Bazel compute rebuild sets.

problems.title: Java Source Formatter — Indentation Fixer
problems.description: Implement a basic Java source code indenter that takes unindented/incorrectly indented Java code and produces properly indented output. Rules: each `{` increases indent by one level (4 spaces), each `}` decreases indent before printing it.
problems.hint1: Process line by line. Track indent level as an integer.
problems.hint2: If a line starts with `}`, decrease indent BEFORE printing. If a line ends with `{`, increase indent AFTER printing.
problems.hint3: Trim each line before indenting it. Use repeat() for generating indent spaces.
problems.constraints: Handle only { and } for indentation. 4-space indent per level. Strip existing indentation with trim().
problems.editorial: Process line by line. Track indent level (int). Before printing: if line starts with '}', decrement indent. Print indent (spaces * level) + trimmed line. After printing: if line ends with '{', increment indent. Handle edge cases: lines with both '{' and '}' (rare in well-formatted code).

problems.title: Type Erasure — Generic Information at Runtime
problems.description: Java's generics are erased at compile time. Demonstrate type erasure by showing that `List<String>` and `List<Integer>` have the same runtime Class, and implement a workaround using a 'Type Token' pattern to preserve generic type information.
problems.hint1: new ArrayList<String>().getClass() == new ArrayList<Integer>().getClass() → true. Type erasure makes them identical at runtime.
problems.hint2: Type Token pattern: create an anonymous subclass of TypeToken<T>. The superclass generic type argument IS preserved in the .class file via getGenericSuperclass().
problems.hint3: Use ((ParameterizedType) getClass().getGenericSuperclass()).getActualTypeArguments()[0] to retrieve the preserved type.
problems.constraints: Type erasure demonstration must show same runtime class. TypeToken must use anonymous subclass trick. Must use getGenericSuperclass().
problems.editorial: At runtime, List<String> and List<Integer> have the same Class object (java.util.List) — generics are erased. To preserve type at runtime, use a type token: abstract class TypeToken<T> { Type getType() { return ((ParameterizedType) getClass().getGenericSuperclass()).getActualTypeArguments()[0]; } }

problems.title: javac Diagnostic Collector
problems.description: Using the javax.tools API, write a method that compiles a Java source string and collects all compilation errors/warnings, returning them as a list of formatted diagnostic messages instead of printing to stderr.
problems.hint1: Use DiagnosticCollector<JavaFileObject> as the diagnostics listener when calling compiler.getTask().
problems.hint2: compiler.getTask(writer, fileManager, diagnosticCollector, options, classes, compilationUnits) returns a CompilationTask.
problems.hint3: After calling task.call(), iterate diagnosticCollector.getDiagnostics() to get all errors/warnings.
problems.constraints: Must use DiagnosticCollector. Must return List<String> of formatted messages, not print to stderr. Requires JDK.
problems.editorial: ToolProvider.getSystemJavaCompiler() + DiagnosticCollector<JavaFileObject> as listener. Pass the collector to getTask(). After compile(), call collector.getDiagnostics() and filter by Diagnostic.Kind. Returns structured error info (line, column, message) rather than raw stderr output.

problems.title: Sealed Class Hierarchy and Pattern Matching
problems.description:
Implement a sealed class hierarchy representing AST nodes for a calculator, then use Java 17+ pattern matching switch to evaluate it without any casting:

```
sealed interface Expr permits Num, Add, Mul, Neg
```

Evaluate: `Add(Num(3), Mul(Num(4), Neg(Num(2))))` = 3 + (4 * -2) = -5
problems.hint1: Use `sealed interface Expr permits ...` and `record Num(int v) implements Expr` etc.
problems.hint2: Pattern matching switch: `switch (expr) { case Num n -> n.v(); case Add a -> eval(a.left()) + eval(a.right()); ... }`
problems.hint3: The compiler enforces exhaustiveness on sealed types — if you miss a case, it's a compile error.
problems.constraints: Must use sealed interface with permits clause. Must use pattern matching switch (Java 17+). No instanceof casts.
problems.editorial: Sealed classes permit a closed set of subclasses — the compiler knows all cases. Pattern matching switch is exhaustive over sealed types without a default clause. Records are ideal as sealed subtypes. Combine with instanceof pattern matching for safe downcasting without explicit casts.

problems.title: Bytecode Instruction Frequency Analyzer
problems.description: Parse the output of `javap -c` for a class and count the frequency of each bytecode instruction mnemonic. Return the top-5 most used instructions. This simulates what JVM profilers do to identify optimization opportunities.
problems.hint1: Parse javap -c output line by line. Bytecode lines match the pattern: `   <number>: <mnemonic> <optional operands>`
problems.hint2: Use regex: `\s+\d+:\s+(\w+)` to extract the mnemonic from each bytecode line.
problems.hint3: Count frequencies in a Map<String, Integer>, then sort by value descending and return top 5.

testCases.input: javap output with iload_1 appearing 3 times

problems.constraints: Must use regex to parse bytecode lines. Return top 5 sorted by frequency descending. Skip non-bytecode lines.
problems.editorial: Read .class bytes, find each opcode by scanning the constant pool and code attributes. Count opcode occurrences in a Map<String,Integer>. Sort by count descending. Most frequent opcodes in real code: aload_0 (load this), invokevirtual (method calls), getfield, putfield, return.

problems.title: Implement a Record Desugarer
problems.description:
Java records are syntactic sugar. `record Point(int x, int y)` compiles to a class with: final fields, canonical constructor, equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and accessor methods x() and y().

Write a `desugarRecord(String recordName, String[] fields)` method that generates the equivalent Java class source code as a String.
problems.hint1: Generate: class declaration with final fields, then canonical constructor, then accessor methods (same name as field, no 'get' prefix), then equals(), hashCode(), toString().
problems.hint2: For equals(): cast other to the record type and compare each field. For hashCode(): use Objects.hash(field1, field2...).
problems.hint3: For toString(): format is `RecordName[field1=value1, field2=value2]` — note square brackets, not parentheses.

testCases.input: Point with int x, int y
testCases.output: contains private final int x; and public int x() { return x; }

problems.constraints: Must generate: final fields, canonical constructor, accessors (no 'get' prefix), equals, hashCode, toString with record bracket format.
problems.editorial: A Java record `record Point(int x, int y){}` desugars to: final class Point with private final fields, canonical constructor, accessor methods x() and y(), equals(), hashCode(), toString(). The compact constructor body runs before field assignment. Generate this equivalent class programmatically.

problems.title: Lambda to Anonymous Class Desugar
problems.description: Java lambdas are compiled using invokedynamic + LambdaMetafactory. Before Java 8, developers used anonymous classes. Write a method that takes a list of integers and applies a transformation using BOTH a lambda and its equivalent anonymous class form, showing they produce identical results.
problems.hint1: A lambda `x -> x * 2` is equivalent to `new Function<Integer,Integer>() { public Integer apply(Integer x) { return x * 2; } }`
problems.hint2: Both implement the same functional interface. The only difference is syntax and performance (lambda uses invokedynamic, anonymous class creates a new .class file).
problems.hint3: Use java.util.function.Function<T,R> as the functional interface.
problems.constraints: Lambda and anonymous class must implement the same transformation. Use Function<Integer,Integer>.
problems.editorial: Lambda `(x) -> x * 2` desugars to an anonymous class implementing the functional interface, with the lambda body as the apply() method. Inner class captures effectively-final variables from the enclosing scope. For method references, the anonymous class delegates to the referenced method.

problems.title: Source Code Minifier
problems.description:
Write a Java source code minifier that removes: all comments (// and /* */), extra whitespace, and blank lines, while preserving all tokens and their order. This simulates what ProGuard/R8 does before obfuscation.

Input: multi-line class with comments and blank lines
Output: single-line equivalent (or minimal) source
problems.hint1: Remove block comments first (/* ... */) using regex with DOTALL flag. Then remove line comments (// ...).
problems.hint2: Replace multiple whitespace characters with a single space. Trim each line.
problems.hint3: Be careful: don't collapse whitespace inside string literals. Use a state machine or regex that excludes string contents.
problems.constraints: Must handle // and /* */ comments. Must collapse whitespace. Warning: string literals may contain // — this simplified version doesn't handle that edge case.
problems.editorial: Remove all comments (line and block). Remove all unnecessary whitespace (multiple spaces → one). Shorten identifiers if safe. Keep string literals intact. Process as a character stream with state tracking (inside string, inside comment). Output should compile identically to the input.

problems.title: Method Signature Parser
problems.description:
Java's reflection API uses JVM method descriptor format. Parse a JVM method descriptor string and return a human-readable signature.

Descriptor format: `(parameter types)return type`
Type codes: Z=boolean, B=byte, C=char, S=short, I=int, J=long, F=float, D=double, V=void, L<classname>;=object, [=array

Input: `(ILjava/lang/String;[D)Z`
Output: `boolean method(int, String, double[])`
problems.hint1: Parse the descriptor left to right. Everything inside `(...)` is parameters. After `)` is the return type.
problems.hint2: For `L...;` types, extract the class name between L and ;, replace / with ., use simple name (last segment after .).
problems.hint3: For arrays `[`, consume the `[` and append `[]` to the next type. Handle `[[` = 2D array.
problems.constraints: Must support all primitive types, void, object (L...;), and array ([). Return type must appear before parameter list.
problems.editorial: Parse method descriptor format: (param_types)return_type. L-prefixed names are object types ending with semicolon. [ prefix is array. B=byte, C=char, D=double, F=float, I=int, J=long, S=short, V=void, Z=boolean. Example: (ILjava/lang/String;)[D → (int, String) → double[].

problems.title: Build a Simple Expression Parser
problems.description:
Extend the tokenizer from a previous problem to build a recursive-descent parser for arithmetic expressions with proper operator precedence (+/- lower than */). Parse and evaluate `3 + 4 * 2 - 1` = 10 (not 13).

Grammar:
```
expr   → term (('+' | '-') term)*
term   → factor (('*' | '/') factor)*
factor → NUMBER | '(' expr ')'
```
problems.hint1: Each grammar rule becomes a method: parseExpr(), parseTerm(), parseFactor(). They call each other recursively.
problems.hint2: parseExpr calls parseTerm, then loops while next token is + or -. parseTerm calls parseFactor, loops for * or /.
problems.hint3: Use an index into a token list. parseFactor handles numbers and parenthesized sub-expressions.
problems.constraints: Must implement operator precedence (* before +). Must handle parentheses. Use recursive descent with separate methods per grammar rule.
problems.editorial: Recursive descent parser: parseExpr() handles + and - (lowest precedence), parseTerm() handles * and / (higher), parseFactor() handles numbers and parenthesized subexpressions. Each level calls the next level for higher-precedence operations, naturally enforcing operator precedence.

problems.title: Full Compilation Pipeline Simulation
problems.description:
Combine the tokenizer and AST parser to build a minimal end-to-end 'compiler' for a tiny subset of Java:
- Variable declarations: `int x = expr;`
- Print statements: `print(expr);`
- Expression: arithmetic with variables

The 'compiler' should: parse the input program, build a symbol table, evaluate/execute it, and print outputs.

Input program:
```
int a = 5;
int b = 3 + a;
print(a * b);
```
Expected output: `40`
problems.hint1: Reuse the tokenizer and expression parser from previous problems. Add variable lookup in parseFactor.
problems.hint2: Parse line by line. Detect 'int' → variable declaration. Detect 'print' → print statement.
problems.hint3: Maintain a Map<String,Integer> as the symbol table for variable values.
problems.constraints: Must support: int declarations with expression RHS, print statement, variable lookup in expressions. Symbol table stored in Map<String,Integer>.
problems.editorial: Full pipeline: source string → tokenize → parse to AST → semantic check (type validation) → generate bytecode representation (instruction list) → execute instructions on a stack machine. Each phase transforms the representation. This mirrors javac's internal phases: scan → parse → analyze → generate.
